data.title: Eli Lilly Buy On Weakness Signal (Technical Analysis)
data.description:
Eli Lilly (NYSE:LLY) just triggered our Timing, Tmg Buy Signal and our computer has calculated a 12-month target of ~$1,150. LLY earnings announcement is due on Oct. 30, and we expect the usual pop after good earnings. Our report below shows the Buy signal in the Tmg column. It also shows another short-term, timing signal in the Stop & Reverse or SAR columns which was just triggered on Friday, shown with a B in the Friday or F column. These signals are similar to the Confirmed buy signal shown in our last article on LLY.

Here is our daily portfolio Watchlist and you can see LLY is on the list with these short-term Buy signals in the Tmg and SAR columns:

Note on the above report that LLY also has a Buy signal in the Fundamental or Fund column. We will check that out below with SA, but right now, we want to turn to the weekly chart and the technical Buy signals.

On the chart below, you can see the vertical, blue line, technical Buy signal triggered by the Full Stochastic or Full STO signal. In addition, you can see the bullish Chaikin money flow signal indicating Demand taking price higher. At the bottom of the chart is the ADX Demand signal. Also notice the uptrend in performance vs. the Index. You can see a bounce after dropping down to test that uptrend that indicates LLY is outperforming the Index.

There are also negative signals helping to create the Supply that is triggering our buy on weakness signals. At the top of the chart is our proprietary Stocks In Demand or SID signal. You can see it has a regular pattern of wide swings from Hold to Buy and back to Hold. We think the reason for this is that many portfolio managers have too much LLY in their portfolio because of the big runup in price. They are probably forced to sell a stock they love every time price moves higher. The buyers, of course, are ETFs, Index funds, hedge funds and small investors.

Here is our weekly chart showing our technical Buy signals, except for the MACD and SID signals:

Now we want to return to our Fund Buy signal mentioned above and shown on our Daily Index Beaters report in the Fund column. We do our due diligence on this signal by checking with SA analysts and Quant ratings. Our SID Hold rating is in agreement with the SA Quant Hold rating, so we are comfortable with that, but we expect earnings will pop our SID signal to a Buy. Both SA and Wall St. analysts rate LLY as a Buy, and we certainly find that supporting the Buy signal in our Fund column shown in the report above.

The SA Quant scores for Growth, Momentum and Profitability are very good for LLY. However, poor scores on Valuation and Revisions are problematic. The forward PE of 68 is difficult to accept and allows for no possibility of any downward revisions. LLY is priced to perfection, and that makes it risky. That may also explain the big drop in our SID score from a Buy signal to a Hold signal.

Poor scores for Valuation and Revisions would prompt many portfolio managers to sell into strength. So even if we get a pop on this earnings report, we will probably see selling on the good news, as we have seen in the past.

Nevertheless, there are other buyers out there who will still be attracted to LLY and the higher target our computer projects for the year ahead. Index funds and sector ETFs usually do not use fundamentals to buy. They only use Demand for the Index to buy LLY because it is part of the Index or ETF.

data.title: Fluor: Stock Looks Bullish To End 2024 And To Begin 2025
data.description:
I'm continuing my coverage of Fluor Corporation (NYSE:FLR) as the company continues to get new contract awards. I last wrote about Fluor in January 2024 where I pointed out how being awarded a steady flow of contracts would have a positive effect on the company. My thesis played out nicely as FLR stock is up 48% since the article was published as compared to the S&P 500's (SPY) gain of 21% over the same time period. I now see Fluor having a strong finish to 2024 which I expect will continue in 2025.

Fluor has a positive outlook as we finish 2024 and head into 2025 with new contract awards. The stock's valuation is still reasonable and Fluor is expected to grow revenue and earnings at a strong pace in 2025. In fact, Fluor's expected earnings growth is expected to be higher at 13% in 2025 as compared to about 6% in 2024. The double-digit EPS growth rate can drive the stock for continued strong gains in 2025.

New Contract Awards as Growth Catalysts

Fluor's steady flow of new contracts demonstrates that demand remains strong for the company's engineering, construction, and related solutions. The most recent contract was awarded to Fluor in August for a $1.4 billion cost plus fixed-fee modification to a previously rewarded contract for fiscal 2025. This contract is for U.S. naval nuclear propulsion work at the Navel Nuclear Laboratory. Work under this contract will be performed in Pittsburgh, PA; Schenectady, NY and Idaho Falls, ID.

FLR was awarded a contract in July from RoPower Nuclear for the next phase of design and engineering of a modular reactor power plant in Romania. The project has a goal of producing carbon-free power which is one of Romania's clean energy initiatives. The amount of the contract was not disclosed in the press release. However, small modular reactors can cost between $50 million and $3 billion depending on the size. This one is designed to be a 462 megawatt reactor which is on the larger end of the scale for small modular reactors. So, the amount of the deal could be closer to $3 billion.

In June, Fluor was awarded a long-term contract with the potential for $30 billion over 20 years. This contract was from the U.S. Department of Energy National Nuclear Security Administration for a joint venture with Fluor, BWX Technologies' (BWXT) subsidiary PanTeXas Deterrence LLC, SOC, and Texas A&M University. This joint venture is for the management and operations of the Pantex Plant located outside of Amarillo, TX. The initial term of the contract is for 5 years, but there is the option to extend it for 3 additional five year periods. Pantex is a key nuclear production plant which is responsible for maintaining the safety, security, and effectiveness of the U.S. nuclear weapons stockpile.

Fluor was awarded a $409 million contract in April from the U.S. Airforce Installation Contracting Agency. This contract is for pavement and transportation support in Tinian, Commonwealth of the Northern Mariana Islands. This is a cost-plus, fixed fee task order with a 60 month period of performance. The project is designed to help strengthen the Air Force's deterrence capabilities.

In April, FLR was awarded a 7-year contract extension from SunCoke Energy for an undisclosed amount. This is for Fluor to support the efficient delivery of coke to SunCoke Energy's steel-making customers. The contract involved SunCoke's five coke plants in the United States.

Also in April, Fluor was awarded a position on the Global Contingency Services Multiple Award Contract III from the U.S. Naval Facilities Engineering Systems Command. FLR was chosen as one of six companies for the indefinite delivery & indefinite quantity contract. FLR is eligible to compete for specific tasks for an amount up to $2 billion for up to 102 months. The contract is to provide timely short-term support services due to natural disasters, military actions, or humanitarian efforts. This would take place in various areas throughout the world.

In March, Fluor was selected for the Hanford Tank Waste Operations & Closure joint venture along with BWX Technologies, and Amentum (AMTM). These companies were selected by the U.S. Department of Energy. The joint venture is to execute the Hanford Integrated Tank Disposition Contract which has an estimated max rate of $45 billion over 10 years. The joint venture is for environmental management operations at the Hanford Site in Washington state.

In February, Fluor was awarded a $525.6 million contract from FEMA. The contract is for recovery services under the Public Assistance Technical Assistance Contracts in the East Zone region. The contract is for one year with four one-year option periods. Under the contract, Fluor is to provide assistance for presidentially declared emergencies/disasters.

All of these developments show that Fluor continues to get a steady stream of contracts for ongoing growth. The strong demand for services have been driving Fluor's margins higher. Fluor's service margins for its traditional EPCM (engineering, procurement and construction management) business have been in the 20% range in 2024. Fluor's shift to an asset light business also helps drive margins higher. This helped drive Q2 2024 net income margin to 4% over the 1.55% net income margin from Q2 2023. The increases in net income margin can drive strong earnings growth for the remainder of 2024 and for 2025.

Fluor also made improvements in its return metrics since I conveyed that weakness in my last article. Fluor's TTM ROE increased from 5.9% in January 2024 to the current TTM ROE of 19%. The ROIC also increased from 6% to 7.5% and the ROA increased from 2.4% to 6% over the same time period. These higher returns can help drive earnings growth going forward.

Valuation

Fluor is trading at 16.9x expected EPS of $3.26 for 2025 (consensus). This is higher than the 14.3x forward PE that FLR was trading at when I wrote my last article back in January. The stock did make strong gains in 2024 which increased the PE. However, FLR still trades below the Engineering & Construction industry's forward PE of 23.5x. So, while FLR's valuation is higher since my last article, the stock is still attractively valued below its industry. This valuation still allows for some PE expansion as Fluor continues to grow.

The consensus estimate EPS of $3.26 for 2025 represents a 13% gain over the expected EPS of $2.88 for 2024. Fluor's strong margin increases can drive EPS growth to achieve these estimates. The increased margins are also driven by FLR's revenue growth. FLR's revenue is expected to increase by 12.5% in 2024 and by about 9% in 2025.

Technical Perspective

I used FLR's weekly chart above to provide a long-term multiple-year perspective. We can see the stock is a nice uptrend with some healthy pullbacks along the way. The stock did touch the overbought area just above 70 on the RSI. However, it is possible for the price to go deeper into overbought territory. Investors should be aware that a pullback could occur at any time. That is nothing to worry about as long as Fluor's fundamentals remain strong.

The stock does tend to pullback after reaching overbought or close to overbought areas. These pullbacks have been followed by strong increases which led to higher highs, driven by FLR's revenue & earnings growth.

The MACD indicator (bottom of the chart) shows positive momentum as the blue MACD line crossed above the red signal line. At the same time, the histogram turned from red to green. So, there could be continued increases in the stock before a pullback occurs. It can be difficult to gauge where the stock will go in the short-term. So, investors should have a long-term perspective as FLR's stock should correlate approximately with earnings growth with the potential for a boost from PE expansion.

Risks to the Investment Thesis

The biggest risk for the company is a slowdown in demand for Fluor's service offerings. FLR's traditional EPCM business is what has been driving the company's margin increases and overall growth. A significant decrease in government spending could lessen demand for some of Fluor's potential contracts. This includes United States and foreign government spending.

Fluor's revenue highly depends on a stream of new contracts. There is no guarantee that FLR will continue to receive a steady flow of new contracts. This depends on FLR's customers' willingness to direct money to these types of projects.

Fluor's Long-Term Outlook

With the United States and other countries returning to a rate cutting cycle, this could stimulate higher spending on some of the large projects that Fluor specializes in. The current positive momentum that Fluor is achieving is likely to drive strong growth for the remainder of 2024 and for 2025. Higher margins are likely to drive earnings growth as demand for Fluor's high margin service business remains strong.

Investors should watch for Fluor to be awarded new contracts in the future to gauge the health of the demand for the company's offerings.

The stock has the potential to increase at a pace that is higher than FLR's earnings growth. I think that there is still some room for multiple expansion as FLR still trades below its industry.

data.title: Hyundai Drives Value Amid Industry Shifts (Ratings Upgrade)
data.description:
Thesis

Hyundai Motor Company (OTCPK:HYMTF) is one of those companies that flies under the radar, quietly going about its business while most of us are distracted by shinier things. Yet it’s easily one of my favorite stocks to own. They’ve managed to crack the code on making cars that offer solid performance and sleek design—all without asking you to remortgage your house to afford one.

But Hyundai’s not just about cars. In 2023, I wrote about how their AI-powered robots are set to alter the playing field along with making significant strides in hydrogen technology, and sustainable mobility. Now, as we get closer to the Q3 2024 earnings, coming out on 10/24, a few things have changed since I last updated and downgraded the stock to a ‘Hold.’ Even with that downgrade, the stock has still kept pace with the 5-6% gain we’ve seen in the S&P 500 (SPY).

In this update, I’m going to take another look at Hyundai’s valuation, dig into the S&P Global warning about the challenges coming for the auto industry, and highlight a few key partnerships. I’ll also break down Hyundai's September and Q3 2024 sales reports to get a better idea of what we can expect from the upcoming earnings. Finally, I’ll update my current rating.

Record Sales Amid Industry Challenges

In Q3 2024, Hyundai Motor America hit a new retail sales record, even with all the economic and industry challenges in play. They moved 210,971 units, up 5% from the same time last year, making it one of the strongest third quarters in the company’s history.

A big part of this growth came from the rising demand for hybrid and electric vehicles, especially models like the Kona and Ioniq 5. These form an essential part of Hyundai’s effort to appeal to an increasing number of green car buyers. Their hybrid lineup really stood out, with a 36% jump in sales in September alone, showing Hyundai’s ability to balance traditional gas engines with a serious push towards electrification.

September Sales Challenges

Despite the solid performance overall, Hyundai’s September sales took a bit of a tumble, down 9% compared to September 2023. It's worth noting that this dip had less to do with any grand market trends and more to do with some rather unremarkable technicalities—fewer selling days and Labor Day deals spilling over into August.

CEO Randy Parker pointed out that while these short-term hiccups hit the September numbers, the overall outlook stays very positive. Hyundai is still on track for a record-breaking year, thanks in large part to the continued success of the IONIQ 5 electric vehicle, which saw a 30% jump in retail sales during Q3.

Hyundai's Long-Term EV Strategy

Hyundai’s recent market success is no fluke—it ties in nicely with their global game plan, which targets selling 5.55 million vehicles a year by 2030, with 2 million of those being electric. To make this happen, Hyundai has been quietly but steadily building up its hybrid and electric lineup, and the numbers back it up in the strong sales of the IONIQ 5 and the record-breaking numbers for the Tucson and Santa Fe hybrids.

What’s particularly smart here is that Hyundai understands the simple truth that not all consumers are on the same page—some see hybrids as a kind of gateway vehicle, while others are diving in, fully committed to the electric cause. Meanwhile, Hyundai’s big investments in production, like the new Metaplant America EV facility in Georgia, underline the company’s focus on ramping up EV production and boosting its presence in the U.S. market.

Challenges in the Broader Auto Market

That said, Hyundai’s success comes with its fair share of industry-wide challenges. A report from S&P Global highlights that the U.S. auto industry is staring down limited growth and rising economic pressures. The report flags some concerns, like modest credit deterioration, especially for lower-rated auto suppliers, and predicts U.S. auto sales will likely stay flat in 2024 at around 15.5 million units and growth is only expected to tick up by 1% to 2% in 2025 and 2026.

On top of that, rising unemployment could cut into consumers’ ability to spend, making it harder for automakers to keep profit margins up. To make matters trickier, average transaction prices for new vehicles are expected to drop by 6% to 8% by 2025, as automakers roll out more incentives to attract budget-conscious buyers. While Hyundai’s lower-priced EVs, like the IONIQ 5, could benefit from this trend, it might also squeeze margins, forcing the company to focus on scaling production and cutting costs to stay profitable.

Slower Growth in the EV Market

S&P Global’s report also points out some bumps in the road for the electric vehicle market. While EV and plug-in hybrid sales are expected to hit 20% of the total market by 2026, growth has slowed in 2024. Many models are dealing with rising inventories and increased competition.

Take Tesla, for example—they’ve struggled to hold onto market share, even after multiple price cuts on the Model 3. This highlights a broader issue: profitability parity between electric and traditional vehicles probably won’t happen until after 2027, especially for automakers that can’t hit economies of scale. For Hyundai, this means they’ll need to keep pushing to cut costs and ramp up production efficiency as they go head-to-head with both established players like Tesla and new competition from China.

Impact of the 2024 U.S. Election

The 2024 U.S. presidential election adds another wrinkle to Hyundai’s strategic planning. Depending on who comes out on top, the regulatory landscape could shift in some pretty significant ways, particularly when it comes to emissions standards, trade policies, and those ever-tempting federal incentives for electric vehicles. According to S&P Global Mobility, a few different paths could unfold based on who wins.

If the Republicans win, we could see emissions regulations more or less put on ice for model years 2028 through 2032. Naturally, this would slow the march toward electrification, forcing Hyundai to put a bit more love into their internal combustion engine and hybrid models, while perhaps easing off the gas (or, in this case, electricity) on their big EV push in the U.S.

And then there’s the matter of federal funding. The Inflation Reduction Act (IRA), which has been helping buyers justify the price tag of EVs through generous incentives, could see its budget slashed. If that happens, demand for Hyundai’s electric lineup—especially the IONIQ series—might take a bit of a hit.

On the flip side, if the Democrats manage to hang onto the White House, the regulatory environment would likely stay favorable for EV growth. In this scenario, Hyundai would likely enjoy the continued benefits of federal incentives, which would help speed up the transition to EVs. We might also see tougher emissions standards coming down the pipeline, nudging automakers to keep cranking out cleaner, more efficient cars.

The way I see it, no matter the outcome, Hyundai’s flexibility and its investments in U.S. production, like the Metaplant America facility, put the company in a strong position to handle changing regulations and evolving consumer demands.

Strategic Partnerships for Technological Advancements

Hyundai, with its characteristic knack for overcoming difficulties, has also been teaming up with the right players to boost its tech capabilities. In September 2024, Hyundai and General Motors did what all sensible companies do when facing a complex future—they signed a Memorandum of Understanding (MOU) to see if they could help each other out. The idea is to explore potential collaborations in areas like electric and hydrogen-powered vehicles.

By pooling their resources, both companies can reduce costs, particularly in securing those all-important materials like batteries and steel—key ingredients in the clean-energy vehicle recipe. This collaboration is expected to speed up Hyundai’s ability to launch new models, while also tackling the rather pesky issue of making electric vehicles affordable on a large scale.

Hyundai's Autonomous Vehicle Ambitions

Hyundai’s venture into autonomous driving is another rather key piece of its long-term growth puzzle. In October 2024, they joined forces with Alphabet’s Waymo, aiming to install Waymo’s sixth-generation autonomous driving technology into Hyundai’s IONIQ 5 SUV. It opens up a nice window for Hyundai to make a bigger splash in the mobility-as-a-service world, with plans to have Waymo-enabled IONIQ 5s cruising around by late 2025.

Valuation and Final Takeaway

To value HYMTF, I took a slightly different route this time, opting to use Hyundai’s compound annual growth rate (OTC:CAGR) of 4.55% from 2014 to TTM rather than the Adjusted (Operating) Earnings Growth Rate.

I also factored in its earnings per share (EPS) of 37.47 and a rather low price-to-earnings (P/E) ratio of 4.59 that suggests the stock is quite undervalued, especially when you consider that the market typically shoots for a P/E closer to 15. So, if Hyundai can keep its earnings on track, there’s plenty of room for growth here.

With an EPS of $37.47, Hyundai’s earnings are quite robust—especially when you realize the stock is hanging out in the low $60s. That said, the 4.55% growth rate might not exactly set anyone’s hair on fire, and those chasing faster returns might well pass it by. But when it comes to fundamentals, Hyundai’s valuation should be appealing.

All things considered, I’m upgrading Hyundai to a ‘Buy’: the stock is clearly undervalued, with a strong EPS and a modest P/E ratio, which leaves plenty of space for growth, provided the earnings keep holding up. And while the industry itself isn’t without its challenges, Hyundai’s strides in EV, robotics, and its strategic partnerships mean it’s positioned rather nicely for long-term success—even if a few short-term uncertainties pop up along the way.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Entegris: The Road Ahead Has Become Misty
data.description:
Entegris (NASDAQ:ENTG), a supplier of materials and process solutions for the semiconductor and other industries, was powering higher earlier in the year, which made it relatively easy to guess the most likely direction of the stock. However, the road ahead has become harder to figure out for ENTG with the appearance of several headwinds, which a leading supplier of lithography systems recently reiterated. The stock has struggled lately, which may portend a possible drop to new lows. ENTG needs to turn it around for the bulls to not get discouraged, especially since ENTG is not without its flaws. Why will be covered next.

ENTG has struggled in October

A previous article from February 2024 mentioned how the stock had rallied higher because it was able to neutralize the negatives by pointing out the positives. Upwards adjustments to ENTG’s financial model, for instance, negated any blowback the stock could have gotten from ENTG missing expectations with its quarterly guidance.

The stock continued higher in an uptrend by moving higher in an ascending channel, but the article noted that a pullback was likely not far away. In addition, ENTG remained heavily leveraged, mostly due to all the debt it acquired as a result of the CMC acquisition, just when the industry entered a downturn. In the end, ENTG was rated a hold.

The chart above shows why the article dodged a bullet by not buying into the rally, even though there was reason to. Notice how the stock was in an uptrend earlier in the year, including when the previous article was written, but ENTG was unable to push higher after early March. ENTG, for instance, has lost about a quarter of its market since the article was written.

The stock experienced a succession of selloffs that wiped out all the early gains and then some. The first selloff was in April, followed by an even larger one in July, and ENTG may be in the process of another one in October. ENTG is now down 13.9% YTD as a result. In addition, there are a couple of interesting things worth mentioning, looking at the charts.

What the charts suggest the stock is likely to do in the short term

For starters, note how the stock topped out twice at close to the same price point months apart. The stock topped out at $146.91 on March 8, followed by a decline that resulted in a low of $120.36 on April 22. ENTG turned north to hit a high of $147.57 on July 12, but then fell off a cliff to a low of $95.54 on August 5. The March high of $146.91 and the July high of $147.57 are four months apart, yet just $0.66 separate the two.

This could be considered a double top, a bearish sign, which proved to be warranted with the drop that followed the second top. The July collapse resulted in a low of $95.54 on August 5, but a bounce got the stock to as high as $119.95 on August 26, only to falter again afterwards. What is interesting here is that the August 26 high of $119.95 is very close to the aforementioned April 22 low of $120.36.

In both instances, the stock proceeded to turn around and move in the opposite direction. What used to be support tends to become resistance and vice versa, which is what seems to have happened here. In other words, resistance is now in place above the stock. On the other hand, while resistance is in place, it seems the same can be said of support.

The previous chart shows how the stock bounced following a drop in the stock from the July high of $147.57 to the August low of $95.54. The July 2024 high of $147.57 was in turn preceded by an extended move upwards that started with the low of $62.24 in December 2022. More importantly, the 38.2% Fibonacci retracement of $62.24 to $147.57 is $94.81. This is close to where the stock bounced at $95.54, which sheds light on why the stock bounced where it did. ENTG hit support.

If the above is correct, then ENTG finds itself between support and resistance. This could make large extended moves difficult, whether up or down, especially in the short term, unless some catalyst appears that powers the stock through resistance, or alternatively, through support. The path of least resistance is for sideways action in the stock.

Still, the price action has favored the downside in October, since the stock is down for the month. The stock has been sliding for most of October and if that continues, a challenge of support could happen. Support is holding, but bulls would probably be more at ease if the stock showed more strength than it has for much of October.

What is weighing on ENTG?

Up until early 2024 ENTG was doing okay with the stock in an uptrend that can be traced back to December 2022. This uptrend in the stock was preceded by an almost year-long decline in the stock in 2022, which was caused by a number of contributing factors, including a downturn in the industry. The resulting drop in demand caused a decline in the top and the bottom line, which came at a very bad time for ENTG because of the untimely acquisition of CMC Materials in mid-2022.

ENTG had leveraged itself heavily to finance the $5.7B acquisition, but servicing debt is much harder to do in a downturn. ENTG took a number of measures in response, which included selling assets to raise proceeds with which to pay off debt. This included the electronic chemical business and the PIM business.

Measures like these helped the stock turn it around. Improved quarterly results and the upgraded financial model mentioned earlier also helped push the stock higher. However, the external environment has become less favorable in 2024, which led to the collapse in July. The initial drop was triggered by reports of possibly new trade restrictions on China relating to semiconductors and everything related to it, which presumably includes equipment and materials from suppliers like ENTG.

Recent reports that some companies like ASML (ASML), seen as a canary in the coal mine, are feeling the adverse effects of trade restrictions imposed by the U.S. government, which included bookings that was less than half the number expected. A drop in bookings for lithography systems from ASML could be a sign companies intend to reduce spending on new production capacity, including new fabs, which is no good for ENTG due to its large exposure to fab construction.

The above caused ENTG to drop by 7.9% on October 15 and not without reason. Reduced trade implies reduced demand. This is something ENTG can do without because it needs all the demand it can get, especially with the debt it has to service because more trade means more sales and an increased ability to service debt.

ENTG itself is still chugging along

Adding to the uncertainty is that not everyone is in the same boat. For instance, some chipmakers like Intel (INTC) have stated they need to cut back on capex, but others like TSMC (TSM) believe they will need to increase capex. It does not help that ENTG itself has been sending mixed signals. For instance, ENTG believes the industry continues to improve overall following the downturn. From the Q2 earnings call:

“2024 continues to be a transition year for the semiconductor market. Industry inventories are normalizing, and fab utilization rates are broadly improving. These recent trends validate that the industry reached the bottom of the cycle in the first quarter of this year. We expect the market will continue to gradually recover in the second half of this year and will accelerate entering 2025.”

Source: ENTG earnings call

On the other hand, ENTG pointed out the recovery was going slower than expected. The outlook was thus lowered.

“we now expect our sales will be approximately $3.3 billion. This modest reduction in our 2024 sales outlook primarily reflects slightly slower-than-expected market recovery in the back half of the year and the negative impact of foreign exchange versus our original assumptions.”

This mixed messaging offset the progress ENTG made. Quarterly earnings, for instance, are on the rise. In the most recent or Q2 FY2024 report, non-GAAP EPS improved to $0.71 on revenue of $812.65M, as shown below. Keep in mind, ENTG divested a number of units. Not only is this skewing the YoY comparisons, but the gains in gross margin can be partially attributed to the absence of units with lower margins.

One of, if not the biggest flaw ENTG has is the amount of debt it has. The good news is that ENTG continues to make progress here. Long-term debt decreased to $4,122.2M in Q2 FY2024, down from $4,577.1M at the start of FY2024, and partially offset by cash and cash equivalents totaling $320M. In comparison, total debt was above $6B two years ago. Most of the reduction can be attributed to the sale of assets.

What will FY2025 bring?

ENTG looks to be on track for non-GAAP EPS of about $3.15 on revenue of $3.3B by the end of FY2024. This would give ENTG a P/E ratio of 32.8x with the stock at $103.23 as of October 18, which is in line with where ENTG has traded on average in recent years. However, figuring FY2025 out is harder because of the uncertainty to the outlook.

If we just extrapolate into the future and assume the recent sequential improvement in quarterly results continues, then FY2025 EPS is estimated to come in at around $4.25. This would lower the P/E ratio to 24.3x. If correct, then ENTG would be in a good position to hit the targets laid out in its financial model, which calls for non-GAAP EPS of $5.00 on revenue of $4.2B in FY2026.

Keep in mind that if demand comes in weaker than expected for whatever reason, whether due to export restrictions or a slower recovery due to companies cutting back on capex, then that might shift the timetable further out. FY2025 is generally expected to be a year of growth for the industry, including by ENTG itself, but something like new export restrictions, if introduced, could add another twist to that dynamic.

Investor takeaways

ENTG continues to slowly get out of the hole it found itself. Total debt has been reduced by about a third or $2B in the last two years, although at the cost of ENTG becoming a smaller company due to the sale of assets. On the other hand, further progress could be harder because there are no more assets that are easily expendable. The low-hanging fruit is gone. If ENTG wants to reduce debt, it likely won’t be through the sale of assets like before. It will have to do it through earnings.

Earnings have been inching higher, and they are expected to continue to do so next year. However, the growth trajectory could be altered by export restrictions. There are already reports from the likes of ASML that export restrictions are starting to affect the numbers negatively. More export restrictions could impact the way 2025 plays out for ENTG. The recovery has been slower than expected, but it could slow even more.

The charts suggest the stock is most likely going sideways in the short term. If ENTG wants to resume the uptrend from early 2024, it will have to break through resistance. On the other hand, there also appears to be some support in place to prevent ENTG from falling much further. Keep in mind both support and resistance can be breached, especially if a catalyst appears like say the U.S. government announcing a new set of export restrictions.

I am therefore neutral on ENTG with the above in mind. The overall environment has deteriorated for ENTG, certainly in comparison to earlier in the year. The prudent move here may be to wait on the sidelines to see whether the stock breaks through resistance or support, which would show whether the bulls or the bears are in the driver seat. At this time, neither of them seem to have an advantage over the other. ENTG could be on hold while this persists.

The Nasdaq slid 1% on Tuesday, and the S&P 500 halted a two-day rally after Dutch semiconductor giant ASML Holding N.V. (ASML) reported disappointing earnings and fell 14%. Wall Street closed in the red Tuesday, with global chip stocks losing over $420B in market value following ASML's forecast for 2025, which came in below expectations. ASML's focus on AI is strong, yet challenges in other segments, including logic and memory, have slowed recovery efforts. These developments raise concerns about the semiconductor supply chain and the impact of export restrictions. ASML's unfavorable outlook for 2025 isn't the only headwind facing the semiconductor industry. Recent export restrictions imposed by the U.S. and Dutch governments have adversely impacted ASML's business in China, reducing its revenue from the region. China's past robust demand for semiconductor technology has been a significant growth driver for companies like ASML, which now anticipates China to account for only 20% of its total revenue next year, a sharp decline from previous quarters. Additionally, the semiconductor market's volatility was exacerbated by ASML's report, which showed a substantial shortfall in third quarter net orders. This has created ripple effects, causing a noteworthy $420 billion drop in market value across global semiconductor stocks, including those of giants like NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA), Advanced Micro Devices, Inc. data.title: Monopoly Master: Why It Seems Pointless To Bet Against Blackstone
data.description:
Introduction

Have you ever heard of The Landlord's Game?

According to its Wikipedia page, it's a board game patented in 1904. The purpose of this realty and taxation game was to educate people about Georgism, which is essentially a single-tax movement that makes the case that the value created by individuals should be owned by them. The economic benefits from land and natural resources, however, should be shared.

The reason I'm bringing this up is because it's the game that inspired the board game Monopoly, which launched in 1935. In case you have looked at the pictures on its Wikipedia page, this may not come as a huge surprise.

Interestingly, the game had rules for two different approaches, anti-monopolist and monopolist. The anti-monopolist rules allowed all players to benefit from wealth creation. The monopolist rules were all based on forcing other players out of the game, which is what the modern version of Monopoly is based on.

With that said, on July 22, I wrote an article titled "Playing Real-Life Monopoly With Blackstone," a company that reminds me of the Monopoly game. Since then, shares of Blackstone (NYSE:BX) have returned 24%, crushing the S&P 500's 6% return by a wide margin.

The ten-year return of the world's biggest private equity giant is now north of 900%, which is a mind-blowing number by any comparison.

In this article, I'll update my thesis, using the company's just-released 4Q24 earnings. On top of massive cash inflows and real estate strength, the company's lending business is now its largest segment, providing it with an even bigger advantage in a world where financial corporations are throwing trillions at private equity and lending opportunities.

So, let's dive into the details!

Credit Takes Blackstone To A New Level

Blackstone is known for its private equity and real estate operations. However, a new industry has emerged, which is private credit. Especially in light of more rules for global banks since the Great Financial Crisis, a lot of demand has shifted to non-bank players with the means to provide credit.

Using the overview from Apollo Global Management (APO), we see that it estimates a total addressable market in private equity of roughly $40 trillion, making it one of the biggest markets on the planet.

This is what I wrote in my prior Blackstone article regarding credit:

Moreover, credit is a fascinating area. Blackstone sees a strong environment, supported by higher demand for investment-grade and non-investment-grade strategies and elevated interest rates.

As reported by Bloomberg, in 3Q24, the credit and insurance arm of the company took in $21.4 billion of inflows. That's more than half of its total inflows. As a result, it's now the biggest segment, with roughly $355 billion in assets under management ("AUM").

This rise allowed Blackstone (and its peers) to become "financial superstores," with income coming from assets that provided relief when the private equity business was struggling.

Now, it benefits from strength across the board.

Blackstone Is Firing On All Cylinders

As its stock price suggests, Blackstone is back. In 3Q24, it generated $1.3 billion in distributable earnings. This is 6% higher compared to the prior-year quarter, as I highlighted in the chart below. It also saw the highest fee-related earnings in two years.

This growth rate was supported by a 5% growth rate in fee-related earnings, which benefitted from the (expected) Federal Reserve easing cycle.

According to Stephen Schwarzman, CEO and Chairman of Blackstone, the company benefits from a "virtuous cycle," in which falling capital costs increase the value of its assets, leading to more transaction activity.

This increased activity fuels investments and asset sales (realization), which support fundraising. Hence, as the market has started to recover, Blackstone has been very aggressive in its capital deployment.

In the past 12 months, it has deployed $125 billion, which makes it one of the most active periods in its history, as it's almost twice as much as it deployed in the 12 months prior to that.

It also saw a 10% year-over-year increase in its AUM. After raising $41 billion through new inflows, it now manages $1.1 trillion. It has a fee-earnings AUM of $820 billion, 12% more compared to the prior-year quarter. Moreover, as I already mentioned, and as we can see below, slightly more than half of its inflows went to Credit & Insurance.

The company's perpetual capital rose by 12% to $435 billion. Perpetual capital does not have a redemption date, meaning it's capital that does not need to be returned to investors. Most of this cash is invested in credit and real estate.

As Blackstone is a company that invests in a lot of long-term investments with limited liquidity, it's great to have elevated perpetual capital, as it makes the firm much more flexible.

Furthermore, the fee-earning part of this capital accounts for almost half of its total fee-earnings AUM.

If we dig a bit deeper, we find a lot of tailwinds benefitting shareholders, as Blackstone doesn't buy "random" assets.

The Many Tailwinds Of Blackstone

Raising funds is one thing. Putting it to work is another thing.

That's where Blackstone shines.

As I wrote in my prior article, Blackstone's BREIT (Blackstone Real Estate Income Trust) real estate vehicle was three times larger than the next five largest nontraded REITs combined!

In the current market environment, a lot of money is put to work in high-growth areas like digital infrastructure. This includes data centers, which are key for the global "AI revolution."

These investments have made BX the largest owner of data centers in the world, owning assets in all major economic hotspots, including Japan, where it bought AirTrunk for $16 billion.

Currently, the business has data centers worth $70 billion, with more than $100 in projects under development. Most companies simply cannot compete with this scale. That's one of the reasons why I keep bringing up the Monopoly game.

Blackstone has reached such a massive scale that new inflows and existing assets allow it to engage in deals that are out of reach for most real estate players - both public and private.

In general, the company benefits from the recovery in commercial real estate. This has led to a drop of more than 90% in repurchase requests for its BREIT, as investors feel comfortable having cash in the world's largest private REIT.

Given Blackstone's track record of outperforming the public REIT index by 50% since its inception almost eight years ago (according to Blackstone), it makes sense that optimism has returned rather quickly.

The key to its outperformance goes beyond data centers, as close to 90% of its assets are concentrated in warehouses and rental houses - in addition to data centers.

Beyond "traditional" real estate investments, the company's insurance business now manages more than $220 billion. That's 24% more compared to last year. One major benefit is its relationship with major insurers (scale and reputation benefits) and other institutional players. In the first nine months of this year, the company placed or originated $38 billion of A-rated credit for private investment-grade clients. That's 70% more compared to last year.

It also launched an EUR 1 billion open-ended infrastructure fund in Europe, which aims to capitalize on the need for infrastructure development, especially in energy and technology.

All of this is great for shareholders.

BX Shareholders Keep Winning

On top of everything we discussed, Blackstone has a capital-light business model, as less than 1% of its AUM comes from its own balance sheet. Even better:

  • It has no net debt (A+ credit rating).
  • It has zero insurance liabilities.
  • It has barely used any equity financing over the past seven years.

This has allowed it to return 100% of its earnings to shareholders over the past seven years. That's close to $30 billion, 14% of its current market cap.

On October 17, it raised its dividend by 4.9% to $0.86 per share per quarter. This translates to a yield of roughly 2%.

As the company aims to return 85% of its distributable earnings to shareholders, we are dealing with a dividend that has been through a number of ups and downs in recent years. However, just like BX's business, the long-term trend is up.

There's just one problem.

Valuation

Using the FactSet data in the chart below, analysts expect the company to grow its EPS by 11% this year, potentially followed by 32% and 16% growth in 2025 and 2026, respectively.

While these numbers are subject to change, they reflect the company's impressive potential.

Unfortunately, the market has priced in a lot of growth, as BX trades at a blended P/E ratio of 40x!

Its 20-year average is 16.1x. In my prior article, I went with a 22.5x multiple to incorporate elevated growth and a favorable outlook. However, even that multiple makes Blackstone look expensive, as we can see in the chart above (the pink line).

Given that Blackstone shares are prone to regular correction (see below), I do not see the need to chase Blackstone at current prices - regardless of its bright future.

If the stock comes down close to 15% (or more), I believe the risk/reward is much better, making it suitable for new long-term investments.

Takeaway

Blackstone continues to outperform, fueled by strength in credit, real estate, and private equity. The firm's scale (it now manages over $1 trillion in assets) allows it to dominate markets like data centers and credit while benefiting from secular long-term tailwinds.

While its fundamentals remain strong, including a growing dividend and a capital-light business model, the stock's current valuation is very lofty.

With a blended P/E of 40x, I'm waiting for a pullback before becoming more optimistic. I believe a correction of around 15% would offer a more favorable entry point for long-term investors.

Given that Blackstone remains prone to interest rate volatility and economic growth, I believe if the Fed refrains from cutting rates this year, we could see some weakness in the private equity space.

Beyond that, regular market corrections and economic growth fears could provide buying opportunities.

data.title: Is NVDY The Smart Play For Nvidia Investors Seeking Income Stability?
data.description:
YieldMax NVDA Option Income Strategy ETF (NYSEARCA:NVDY) offers investors an opportunity to generate high income by actively trading options on Nvidia Corporation (NVDA) stock. The fund distributes monthly income to its investors, which currently yields up to 55%. This approach leverages Nvidia's high price volatility to maximize option premiums, balancing downside risks with high-income potential. The fund is part of the YieldMaxETF family, which offers similar ETFs on stocks like AMZN, TSLA, and AAPL.

Launched in May 2023, the fund is still in its early stages and currently manages assets totaling $1.23 billion. Over the past year, it has achieved a total return of 145% for its investors, which includes the amounts distributed monthly. Although NVDY's performance has not matched that of NVDA during this period, it is crucial to recognize that these are distinct types of investments with varying objectives. NVDA is primarily a growth stock, whereas NVDY is structured to offer consistent income. Therefore, a direct comparison of their returns is not appropriate.

This article will examine NVDY's investment approach, its past performance, and prospects for future expansion. Additionally, we will evaluate whether NVDY serves as a feasible alternative to holding NVDA common stock.

Investment Strategy

The strategy of NVDY is fairly simple: generate income by actively trading options on NVDA and distribute this income to the investors. The applied strategies are income-focused option strategies, which give up the upside potential of NVDA stock and in exchange receive a net premium.

The option strategies YieldMax is applying mainly fall under 2 categories:

1) Standard Strategy: Selling Synthetic Covered Calls on the underlying stock. This means the fund will sell put options on the underlying stock and simultaneously buy call options to simulate a synthetic long position in the stock. Once this is done, a call option is written on this position to generate option income by collecting the premium of the sold call option.

2) Opportunistic Strategy: Selling Credit Call Spreads. Here the fund will sell a call option with a given strike price and will simultaneously buy a call option with a higher strike price in the same maturity period.

Both strategies are comparable in the fact that they have limited profit potential and will benefit if the underlying stock price remains stable or appreciates. To complement the options strategy, the fund will also make investments in short-term US Treasuries.

The fund currently invests 94% in the cash & treasuries, with the remaining part invested in the option strategies.

The fund's current holdings include a credit call spread maturing on October 24th, 2024. The call option with a strike price of $142 has been sold and the call with a strike price of $148 has been bought. This position will benefit maximally if the stock price of NVDA remains below the $142 level until October 24th. On this credit spread, the fund currently has a gain of $9.47 million (the sum of the 2 market values in the table here below).

The income from these options trades is then distributed to the investors of the fund on a recurring base. The most recent distribution of NVDA was $1.09 which represents a 55% distribution yield on annualized basis. Throughout 2024, the distributions have been relatively high, offering investors a decent yield on their NVDY holdings.

Historical Performance

These option trades can yield elevated ROIs to their investors, but we need to keep in mind their upside potential is limited. This is not the case when an investor would simply buy NVDA stock, the upside here is unlimited and has been stellar in the past (+2,800% in the past 5 years). When we compare the performance of an investment in NVDA and NVDY over the past year, we can see that NVDA has been the stronger performer.

This should not come as a surprise, NVDA has been Wall Street's favorite in the past year and the stock keeps rising to new high levels. NVDY has a different strategy, they aim to lock in profits by trading options on the underlying stock and they delivered 145% with their strategy in the past year.

Let's have a look at the downside risk of both assets, by focusing on the period June 17 - July 30th in 2024. During this period, NVDA declined by 21% and NVDY lost 15.4% of its value. As we would have expected, NVDY declines in value as soon as NVDA starts to decline, but the total decline was less outspoken as it was mitigated by the generated option income.

One key aspect that will drive the P&L profile of the options trades is the underlying (implied) volatility of NVDA. The higher this volatility, the more uncertainty investors are anticipating about the future stock price of NVDA and the higher the option premiums will be. NVDY will directly benefit from high volatility, as this will allow it to generate higher returns from the option trades.

Examining NVDA's implied volatility over the past year reveals that it has consistently remained elevated, fluctuating between 40% and 60%. When implied volatility approaches these limits, options start appearing fairly expensive in the market. This is one of the reasons for such distributions from NVDA to its shareholders.

High implied volatility creates conditions very favorable to the strategy of NVDY, which may profit from higher option premiums to generate significant income. The relationship between market volatility, option pricing, and fund distributions emphasizes how NVDY can maintain attractive payouts for its shareholders.

The chart above shows that implied volatility typically spikes around the quarterly earnings announcement dates. During these periods, market participants are expecting above-average volatility in the stock price of NVDA.

Future Outlook

The main question now is: should we invest in NVDY? Can NVDY offer a better risk/reward ratio compared to a standard investment in NVDA shares?

Investors who are bullish on NVDA and anticipate the stock still has much more upside potential would be better off investing in NVDA common shares directly. This investment will yield superior returns, as long as NVDA's market value keeps growing. For me personally, I would take a conservative approach here, as NVDA's market value is currently $3,385 bln which makes it the second highest in the world (after AAPL). NVDA's earnings have been impressive, but like any stock, its price is influenced by market sentiment and has potential limits.

Investors with a moderately bullish outlook on NVDA may find NVDY to be a more attractive option. This ETF can profit from fluctuations in NVDA's stock price, allowing for income generation even without significant upward price movement. Even if the stock price of NVDA would remain relatively flat, the ETF would still benefit from the income on the performed option trades, as long as the underlying volatility remains decent.

Seeking Alpha's current Quant Rating implies a "Hold" status for NVDA, which is in line with my personal view, making NVDY the better investment alternative.

Risks & Considerations

Investing in NVDY comes with risks, just like any other option-income ETF. If NVDA's stock price takes a big dive, even NVDY's advanced options strategies won't completely shield you from losses. While the options income can help cushion the blow, your overall investment will still feel the impact.

Another thing to keep in mind is the underlying volatility of NVDA. The options premiums that NVDY collects are closely tied to how much NVDA is expected to fluctuate. If things calm down, the income from the options trades will likely decline and the distributions can be lowered.

Conclusion

NVDY is a great option for investors who believe in the potential of NVDA and want to earn extra cash via recurring distributions. Throughout 2024, these distributions have been exceptionally strong, but the same can be said for the stock return of NVDA.

While the fund is exposed to downside risk, the period between June and July 2024 demonstrated how NVDY's option strategies can help mitigate this risk. Investors should be mindful of the downside risks of these options strategies, such as declining volatility and the potential for substantial losses, especially if the underlying stock experiences a sharp decline.

Overall, we recommend NVDY shares for investors who maintain a bullish outlook on NVDA and seek to benefit from monthly income generated through option strategies.

data.title: BME: Defensive Healthcare Exposure With A Tender Offer Opportunity
data.description:
Written by Nick Ackerman, co-produced by Stanford Chemist

BlackRock Health Sciences Trust (NYSE:BME) is a closed-end fund providing investors exposure to a portfolio of traditional healthcare companies. The fund also then utilizes a covered call strategy to overwrite a portion of its underlying portfolio. This is in an effort to earn additional option premiums to help support its monthly distribution along with harvesting gains from its underlying portfolio. It's also a slightly defensive strategy, which makes the fund a bit more defensive alongside its underlying portfolio.

Since our last update, BME hasn't moved too much in terms of total returns. The discount during this period had widened slightly but has since narrowed back a bit.

CEF discounts/premiums are always important, but this, in particular, is even more important because BME is one of the many BlackRock closed-end funds with a discount management program put in place that can trigger tender offers. For BME, they just recently qualified for the latest round, but it was quite close. That said, the discount is still attractive and having the opportunity to participate in the tender offer could provide some additional upside.

BME Basics

  • 1-Year Z-score: -0.38
  • Discount/Premium: -7.50%
  • Distribution Yield: 6.15%
  • Expense Ratio: 1.07%
  • Leverage: N/A
  • Managed Assets: $604.5 million
  • Structure: Perpetual

BME's investment objective is "total return through a combination of income, current gains, and long-term capital appreciation." They will attempt to achieve this by a pretty simple investment policy - "under normal market conditions, at least 80% of its assets in equity securities of companies engaged in the health sciences and related industries and equity derivatives with exposure to the health sciences industry."

Tender Offer Potential

Earlier this year, a number of BlackRock CEFs announced a discount management program, which included BME. This came along with four different measurement periods over the course of the year after the announcement.

If any fund averaged a discount of -7.50% or greater during this period, the fund would conduct a 2.5% tender offer at 98% of NAV. The 2.5% may seem like a small portion, but not everyone participates in these tender offers, meaning it leaves more shares to be repurchased from investors for those who do participate. Given that we have a total of four measurement periods, that also means up to a total of 10% may end up being repurchased through these tenders.

The first tender offers wrapped up in August, and for BME, it worked out particularly well. Here is how I fared with BME during the first round.

BlackRock Health Sciences Trust (BME) saw the best proration I had in this round of offerings, with a total of ~27.27% of shares accepted at a price of $44.4822. BME was already one of my largest positions, but I added even more than what was accepted anyway, thanks to what I view as a very compelling discount that the fund is trading at. I picked the shares up at $41.96.

As we can see, the proration ended up meaning that the number of shares accepted for the tender was nearly 11x the 2.5% tender offer.

With that, the second measurement period results were announced and BME made the list once again. However, it was just barely at an average discount of -7.63% during that period.

The expiration to participate in the tender offer for BME is on November 18. Brokers may have different internal cut-off period, which they should have listed themselves, but if it isn't, then contacting your broker is a good idea.

Now, it's never guaranteed that the second round will be as successful. More investors could participate, or the market could decline sharply, making it less appealing for investors to participate if they do so at a loss. For me, a gain/loss isn't the relevant metric, as the idea would be to repurchase the shares afterward.

Even realizing a loss doesn't mean that I can't sell a small portion for 98% of NAV, which could still be higher than the market price, and then repurchase for lower. That said, I know some investors avoid realizing losses.

The reverse of this, which is also another consideration, is potentially realizing a capital gain in a taxable account. That could make a deal less attractive to consider as well if the discount narrows meaningfully; then, realizing capital gains on top of that could potentially negate any potential opportunity.

The fund's discount has narrowed a bit since this measurement period as well, so this will be one to keep a close eye on heading into the offering. On the other hand, I believe the discount is still attractive, in general, so accumulating a position here has appeal whatever the outcome going forward. As we can see, the fund has traded at premium levels for most of its life, it has only been now more recently (and around the GFC) where that has been coming down.

In general, CEF discounts had been narrowing more broadly, but BME seems to be going in a different direction in terms of its discount/premium.

Monthly Distribution - Modest But Steady

One of the reasons that BME could be seeing its discount widen is that relative to some other healthcare CEF peers, they aren't paying as high of a distribution. That said, personally, I don't mind the 6.15% distribution rate currently as it has been an incredibly steady payout as this is one fund that has never cut its distribution. They paid a quarterly distribution until around 2014, then switched to a monthly distribution but maintained the same equivalent payout.

With a more modest distribution, there comes a lesser degree of risk for a potential distribution cut-even if the fund has to cover it through significant capital gains, as is the case with BME. That is generally the case for most equity-focused CEFs anyway, so BME isn't too different on that front.

Net investment income coverage as of their last semi-annual report came to less than 5%.

With a lower distribution rate, though, it just means they generally have an easier time hitting the ~5.75% NAV distribution rate compared to another healthcare fund like Tekla Healthcare Opportunities Fund (THQ) with its 9.61% NAV rate. Whatever isn't paid out is going to be "retained" by the fund and added to NAV, which could see potential appreciation over time. BME has been able to deliver on that as well, with its NAV coming up close to doubling since its inception.

The tax classifications of the fund's distributions have often been long-term capital gains or return of capital. A small portion was listed as NII, but the portion that was for 2023 was qualified income. Earlier this year, in a prior update, we touched on the tax classifications further:

For tax purposes, the fund has generally characterized the distribution primarily as long-term capital gains and qualified dividends. That's tax-friendly and makes it likely appropriate for holding in a taxable account.

For 2023, we even see some return of capital in the distribution, which can also be beneficial for deferring tax obligations as it reduces one's cost basis. Some of the ROC was destructive in 2023 as healthcare didn't perform the greatest, and that weakness also showed up in BME with a 3.5% total NAV return for the year.

That said, ROC is not a common classification in the fund's distribution either, so over the long term, one probably shouldn't rely on that tax-deferred component.

BME's Portfolio

The fund's latest semi-annual report put turnover at just 9%, which actually puts it on pace to be very low this year relative to prior full fiscal years. For example, 2023 saw turnover come in at 43%, 2022 was at 41%, and 2021 came in at 49%. With that, we haven't seen too much in terms of dramatic shifts this year for the fund's holdings.

The fund is fairly diversified, but a few of the largest holdings do carry a particularly heavy weighting in the fund. That includes UnitedHealth Group (UNH) and Eli Lilly and Company (LLY) as the top two that come to an allocation of nearly 18% by themselves. Boston Scientific Corp (BSX) is also in a fairly hefty position at a nearly 6% weighting, which then pushes the top three positions to account for nearly 24% of the fund's exposure.

In our prior update, these were the same three top positions, but UNH had slid down to around a 7.8% weighting while LLY held the top spot at 9.84%. UNH had a series of unfortunate events that were pushing the price lower- we outlined those in a prior update because it was one of the causes that was pressuring BME significantly. So, we've already seen the negatives that can happen when a fund is carrying such a concentrated position in some underlying names.

That said, since that time, UNH has come roaring back. At one point, it was then LLY having some challenges in terms of its share price, where the shares dropped to a loss for the year. However, that was fairly short-lived, and both UNH and LLY ultimately performed quite well during this 3-month period between updates. UNH slightly outperformed and was able to nab its stop spot back once again in this fund.

Since then, UNH has more recently announced its earnings and shares slipped lower. While the company beat on both the top and bottom line estimates for the latest quarter, guidance for 2025 was a bit weaker than analysts were originally expecting.

Conclusion

With the second discount measurement period passing on the BlackRock discount management program, we once again have a potential opportunity to capitalize on the tender offer. During this latest round, it was quite close as the fund's discount narrowed since the first measurement period. Then, the discount has since narrowed even further.

Whether or not it makes sense to participate in this next tender offer will be something to watch closely. If the discount narrows further, the potential opportunity will also be reduced-with tax liabilities also potentially being a factor for some investors that will need to be taken into consideration as well.

That said, I believe that BME continues to trade at a relatively attractive discount level compared to where it has historically traded. Further, I like the underlying portfolio, so that makes it quite appealing overall, even if the tender offer wasn't available.

data.title: ACV: This Fund Is Now Covering Its Distributions And Is Worthy Of Consideration
data.description:
The Virtus Diversified Income & Convertible Fund (NYSE:ACV) is a closed-end fund that investors can purchase in order to achieve a very high level of current income. The fund certainly manages to do pretty well at this, which is generally the case with most Virtus-run hybrid convertible bond funds. As of the time of writing, the shares of this fund yield 9.96%, which is far higher than the yields of any of the domestic common stock or bond indices:

The yield of the Virtus Diversified Income & Convertible Fund is also higher than that of most of its peers. We can see this here:

We can clearly see that, with the exception of the RiverNorth Opportunities Fund, the Virtus Diversified Income & Convertible Fund has the highest yield among its peers. This is something that could potentially improve the fund's perception in the eyes of potential investors. After all, the higher yield relative to peers means that investors in this fund will receive a higher level of income for a given level of investment. However, the fund's high yield could also be a sign that the market doubts its ability to sustain the payout going forward. The market has a tendency to price the fund's shares at a level that will give it a competitive yield after it cuts to the level that the fund expects it to pay, after all. This may be especially relevant with Virtus funds, as a few of the other convertible bond funds run by this manager have cut their payouts over the past two or three years. We will be sure to have a close look at this over the remainder of this article.

As regular readers can likely remember, we previously discussed the Virtus Diversified Income & Convertible Fund in late July of this year. The markets in general have been pretty strong since that time, due to a combination of interest rate cuts by various central banks around the world and the expectations of many investors that generative artificial intelligence will result in a new growth wave that rivaled the Internet expansion of the late 1990s. While I somewhat doubt that artificial intelligence will live up to its hype, this belief has given investors something to believe in and provided capital gains to market participants. As such, we can probably expect that the Virtus Diversified Income & Convertible Fund will have delivered a fairly respectable return since our last discussion.

This is indeed the case, as shares of the Virtus Diversified Income & Convertible Fund have appreciated by 5.55% since the previous article was published:

We can, unfortunately, see that this fund failed to keep pace with the S&P 500 Index over the period. This is unsurprising though since a hybrid fund such as this one tends to include both common stocks and bonds. While bonds did pretty well during this period as well (the Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index was up 1.06%), the presence of the bonds still drags down the overall performance of the fund. In any case, a 5.55% gain in less than three months is still very reasonable.

However, as I stated in my previous article on this fund:

A simple look at a closed-end fund's price performance does not necessarily provide an accurate picture of how investors in the fund actually did during a given period. This is because these funds tend to pay out all of their net investment profits to the shareholders, rather than relying on the capital appreciation of their share price to provide a return. This is the reason why the yields of these funds tend to be much higher than the yield of index funds or most other market assets.

The distributions represent a real return on investment that is not captured by a chart of the share price performance alone. When we take this into consideration, we get the following alternative to the chart above:

This certainly changes the overall performance picture here. As we can clearly see, investors in the Virtus Diversified Income & Convertible Fund actually did better than they would have done simply by buying an S&P 500 Index fund. Everybody likes to beat the market, so this will likely be appealing to most potential investors.

Naturally, though, past performance is no guarantee of future results. We should have a look at this fund's positioning and the industry fundamentals in order to come up with a reasonable projection of where the fund might be in the future. The remainder of this article will focus on analyzing its positioning, finances, and updating our thesis as appropriate.

About The Fund

According to the fund's website, the Virtus Diversified Income & Convertible Fund has the primary objective of providing its investors with a very high level of total return. This makes a lot of sense given the fund's strategy, which is explained on the website:

The Fund seeks to provide total return through a combination of current income and capital appreciation, while seeking to mitigate the risk of capital loss. The Fund strives to dynamically allocate across convertibles, equities, and income-producing securities.

The Fund will normally invest at least 80% of its net assets (plus any borrowings for investment purposes) in a diversified portfolio of convertible securities, income-producing equity securities and income-producing debt and other instruments of varying maturities, of which at least 50% of total managed assets are invested in convertibles. The Fund has the latitude to write covered call options on the stocks held in the equity portion.

Attempts to dampen volatility, relative to an equity-only portfolio, due to the asymmetric risk/return features historically exhibited by convertible securities.

The website does not specifically state how much of the fund must be invested in common equities or in traditional bond debt. However, the 50% allocation to convertibles suggests that it will usually be under 50% allocated to common stocks, and potentially quite a bit less. The phrasing of the second paragraph is such that the fund could be anywhere from 50% to 100% in convertibles, and anywhere from 0% to 50% in common stock or traditional bonds.

The fund's most recent semi-annual report states that the Virtus Diversified Income & Convertible Fund was holding the following asset allocation as of July 31, 2024:

Asset Type

% of Net Assets

Corporate Bonds and Notes

Written Options

-0.0%

This is a bit different than what we saw the last time that we looked at this fund. As regular readers may remember, in the previous article, I shared the fund's asset allocation as of April 30, 2024. Here is a summary of the changes from period to period:

Asset Type

% of Net Assets As Of April 30, 2024
